{"step_by_step_thinking": "The patient is a 17-year-old woman with secondary amenorrhea, previously regular menses starting at age 13. She denies sexual activity and has a negative urine pregnancy test. On exam, she has enlarged cheeks (moon facies), calluses on the dorsum of her hands, and reports stress and binge eating. These findings are consistent with an eating disorder, particularly bulimia nervosa or functional hypothalamic amenorrhea (FHA).

Moon facies (enlarged cheeks) and calluses on the hands are classic signs of bulimia nervosa. Document [18] specifically mentions that dental erosions may be present with bulimia nervosa. This directly supports option C: erosion of tooth enamel.

Option A (malar rash) is associated with systemic lupus erythematosus, not bulimia or eating disorders.
Option B (increased blood glucose level) may occur in diabetes, but there is no mention of diabetes symptoms or elevated glucose in the context of eating disorders or bulimia.
Option D (elevated estrogen levels) is inconsistent with the clinical picture; amenorrhea in this context is due to hypothalamic dysfunction from energy deficiency, not elevated estrogen.

Therefore, the most expected finding in this patient, given the history and physical exam, is erosion of tooth enamel.", "answer_choice": "C"}
